nativeRange.habitat: Patches of fine sand on seaward reefs and rubble flats; typically found at depths of 4–40 m; shares burrows with alpheid (pistol) shrimp

decorPreferences: sand substrate (2–4 mm grain, 10 cm depth)

diet.notes: Generally accepts a wide range of meaty foods; has been documented eating mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped krill, and other carnivore preparations. Feeding small amounts two to three times daily is commonly advised.

summary: The Tangaroa Goby is a small, peaceful saltwater fish from the Indo-Pacific that forms a natural partnership with pistol shrimp, sharing a burrow on sandy reef flats. It is generally considered reef safe and typically accepts a variety of frozen and prepared meaty foods without difficulty.
careNotes:
The Tangaroa Goby (Ctenogobiops tangaroai) is a semi-translucent white fish marked with rows of orange spots interspersed with small whitish to bluish spots and a notably elongated first dorsal fin ray. In the aquarium trade it is also sold under the names Tangaroa Shrimpgoby, Tangaroa Prawn Goby, and Masted Shrimpgoby. The type locality is Tutuila Island, Pago Pago, Samoa.

It is well suited to reef aquariums of 75 L or more with a sandy substrate at least 10 cm deep and loose rubble to support burrow construction. In the wild, Tangaroa Gobies share burrows with alpheid shrimp — the small, snapping crustaceans commonly called pistol shrimp — and pairing with a compatible pistol shrimp in captivity is commonly encouraged, as it tends to bring out natural foraging and signaling behaviors.

The species has been known to quarrel with conspecifics, particularly in smaller tanks, so keeping a compatible male-female pair rather than multiple males is generally advised. It tends to remain near the substrate and burrow entrance, venturing into the water column to feed. A tight-fitting lid or screen cover is strongly recommended, as gobies of this genus are considered a high escape risk and have been documented jumping through small gaps.

No successful captive breeding of this species in home aquariums has been reported.
breedingNotes: Has not been successfully bred in home aquariums based on available reports.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: The primary source URL supplied by the controller (collections/gobies) did not list this species directly; the species-specific LiveAquaria page was located via web search and used as primary.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6.0 cm TL), native range, habitat, temperature preference (24.8–29°C mean 27.9°C), trophic level (3.3 = carnivore), taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), IUCN Least Concern status.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (6 cm), temperature (22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), peaceful temperament, pair-keeping recommendation, high escape risk, no captive breeding reports. Size and temperature slightly disagree with LiveAquaria (LiveAquaria: 2 in / 5.08 cm, 72–78°F); averaged across sources.

additional.name: Fishes of Australia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size up to 6.5 cm TL, distribution (Australia GBR/Coral Sea/Scott Reef, Samoa, PNG, Solomon Islands, Marshall Islands), habitat (sandy and rubble areas on reef flats and slopes, 2–40 m), other names (Masted Shrimpgoby). Size 6.5 cm is slightly larger than FishBase 6.0 cm; all three secondary sizes averaged with LiveAquaria to set range 4.0–6.5 cm.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Confirmed SG range (1.023–1.026), temperature (76–80°F = 24.4–26.7°C), KH (8–12 dKH), peaceful temperament, reef compatibility, and noted high escape risk for this genus. SG upper end (1.026) slightly higher than LiveAquaria (1.025); used broader range 1.020–1.026 to average.

fish.breedingNotes: No successful captive breeding in home aquariums has been reported for this species.
